title: Non-Hermitian skin effect induced by spatial noncommutativity
abstract: In all known schemes for the non-Hermitian skin effect, the non-Hermitian ingredient that drives the skin localization, whether asymmetric hopping or gain and loss, is invariably introduced by hand as an independent model parameter along the skin direction. Here we show that when two spatial coordinates do not commute, the skin effect can break free of this paradigm: a gain-loss potential applied along one coordinate automatically generates non-reciprocity along the other through the coordinate noncommutativity, driving all eigenstates to pile up exponentially at a boundary. We term this phenomenon the noncommutative skin effect. The inverse skin length is proportional to the noncommutativity parameter and is given by an analytic formula, exact in the thermodynamic limit and verified by exact diagonalization of lattice models; the reflection symmetry of the imaginary potential furnishes an exact criterion for the presence or absence of the effect, valid rigorously for finite-size systems. For a sinusoidal imaginary potential, the skin direction of all eigenstates flips collectively at parameter points fixed purely by geometry. Because the flip point is independent of the potential strength, the reversal constitutes a zero-crossing measurement scheme intrinsically robust against systematic errors, from which the noncommutativity parameter can be extracted directly. The qualitative transition of the eigenstates from uniform to exponentially localized renders the effect a nonperturbative probe of spatial noncommutativity, and the Peierls-phase structure of its lattice model is in principle accessible to cold-atom synthetic dimensions, photonic resonators, and topolectrical circuits.
summary: The paper introduces the Noncommutative Skin Effect (NCSE), showing that spatial noncommutativity alone can induce boundary localization typically associated with gain/loss terms. By analyzing lattice models and deriving effective 1D Hamiltonians, it establishes rigorous criteria—particularly based on potential symmetry—for the existence of this effect.
main_problem: To demonstrate that spatial noncommutativity can induce the Non-Hermitian Skin Effect (NHSE) without requiring an independent gain/loss term along the skin direction.
main_result:
The phenomenon, termed the noncommutative skin effect (NCSE), is characterized by a skin length inversely proportional to the noncommutativity parameter ($\kappa_
u \propto 	heta$).
method: The analysis uses mathematical transformations (like the Bopp shift) and asymptotic expansions (Taylor/adiabatic approximations) on effective 1D Hamiltonians derived from the 2D noncommutative lattice model.
model_or_system: The system is a quantum mechanical model on a two-dimensional space where spatial coordinates do not commute, $[\hat{x}, \hat{y}] = i	heta$. The Hamiltonian involves kinetic terms and an imaginary potential $V(\hat{y})$.
key_observables:
Inverse skin length ($\kappa_
u$), spectral non-reciprocity ($\epsilon_
u(k) 
eq \epsilon_
u(-k)$), and the collective reversal of eigenstate direction at geometric 'flip points'.
important_parameters: Noncommutativity parameter ($	heta$), lattice size ($L_x, L_y$), and potential strength/geometry.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximations such as the adiabatic limit and Taylor truncation to derive solvable effective 1D equations from the full 2D problem.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the 'Sliding-window mechanism' showing momentum shearing into coordinate space, and numerical verification of exponential envelope localization ($\propto e^{	heta m}$) for various potentials.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the general noncommutative framework, deriving effective 1D models (Hatano-Nelson type), proving existence criteria based on potential symmetry, and finally applying these results to specific lattice potentials (quadratic, sine/cosine).
why_interesting: This work provides a fundamental mechanism linking geometric properties of spacetime (noncommutativity) directly to observable quantum phenomena like topological edge states, which is highly relevant for designing synthetic dimensions in AMO platforms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
